perturbation.original_text: Buyer agrees that all copies of materials and data provided to Buyer (and any information derivative of such information) shall also be “Confidential Information ”; and all Confidential Information shall be returned to Broker in the event that Buyer decides not to pursue the Transaction .
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer agrees that copies of materials and data provided to Buyer (and any information derivative of such information) may be considered “Confidential Information ”; and Confidential Information should be returned to Broker if Buyer decides not to pursue the Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states a clear obligation: all copies of materials are considered confidential information and must be returned. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using "may be considered" and "should be returned," making both the classification of information and the act of returning it discretionary. This weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets, requiring reasonable efforts to maintain their confidentiality. The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain trade secret status under CUTSA. By making the classification of information and its return discretionary, the agreement does not guarantee sufficient protection, potentially jeopardizing trade secret status.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'In consideration of Broker'
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity by using "may be considered" and "should be returned," making the classification of information and the act of returning it discretionary. This undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain trade secret status under CUTSA, as explained in the Law Explanation. Snippet 1, CIV 3426.1, defines 'improper means' as including 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' By weakening the obligation to protect confidential information, the modified text creates a potential loophole where the buyer's actions might not be considered a breach, thus contradicting the intent of CUTSA to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer shall be legally responsible for the actions and omissions of Buyer’s advisors and affiliates.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer may be responsible for actions and omissions of Buyer’s advisors and affiliates based on certain circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that the buyer is legally responsible. The modified text introduces ambiguity with "may be responsible" and "based on certain circumstances," creating uncertainty about the extent of the buyer's liability. This ambiguity makes it harder to enforce the responsibility.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Agency Law, particularly Civil Code § 2338, generally holds a principal (here, the Buyer) responsible for the agent's (advisor's/affiliate's) actions within the scope of their authority. The modified text introduces ambiguity and uncertainty, contradicting the principle of agency law where the principal is typically held accountable for actions done on their behalf.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'In consideration of Broker'
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 2338. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. AGENCY [2295 - 2400] ( Title 9 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. Agency in General [2295 - 2357] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 3. Mutual Obligations of Principals and Third Persons [2330 - 2339] ( Article 3 enacted 1872. ) 2338. Unless required by or under the authority of law to employ that particular agent, a principal is responsible to third persons for the negligence of his agent in the transaction of the business of the agency, including wrongful acts committed by such agent in and as a part of the transaction of such business, and for his willful omission to fulfill the obligations of the principal. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the Buyer's responsibility for their advisors' and affiliates' actions, while the original text clearly states the Buyer 'shall be legally responsible'. California Civil Code § 2338 states that 'a principal is responsible to third persons for the negligence of his agent in the transaction of the business of the agency.' By using 'may be responsible' and 'based on certain circumstances,' the modified text weakens the Buyer's legally mandated accountability and contradicts the principle that principals are responsible for their agents' actions, creating a legal contradiction as the obligation becomes ambiguously enforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer will not circumvent Seller and/or Broker by contacting any person or persons involved with the Business including, without limitation , landlords, employees, suppliers or customers .
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer will try to avoid circumventing Seller and/or Broker by contacting persons involved with the Business to the best of their ability.
perturbation.explanation: The original text is a strict prohibition on circumvention. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing "will not circumvent" with "will try to avoid circumventing… to the best of their ability." This subjective standard weakens the obligation and allows for potential circumvention under the guise of lacking ability, thereby impacting the broker's/seller's exclusive representation and ability to control the sale process.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code, Real Estate Brokerage Relationships
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10176(f) of the California Business and Professions Code prohibits real estate licensees from engaging in conduct that constitutes fraud or dishonest dealing. Actively circumventing the broker, even under the guise of "trying to avoid," could be interpreted as dishonest dealing, especially if it deprives the broker of their commission or undermines their exclusive representation agreement. The weaker language in the modified text makes it more difficult to prove a violation, thus contradicting the intent of the law to prevent dishonest dealing in real estate transactions.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'USE INFORMATION FOR EVALUATION PURPOSE ONLY'
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 10176 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 4. REAL ESTATE [10000 - 11506] ( Division 4 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) PART 1. LICENSING OF PERSONS [10000 - 10580] ( Part 1 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) CHAPTER 3. Real Estate Regulations [10130 - 10249.93] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) ARTICLE 3. Disciplinary Action [10175 - 10186.9] ( Article 3 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) 10176. The commissioner may, upon his or her own motion, and shall, upon the verified complaint in writing of any person, investigate the actions of any person engaged in the business or acting in the capacity of a real estate licensee within this state, and he or she may temporarily suspend or permanently revoke a real estate license at any time where the licensee, while a real estate licensee, in performing or attempting to perform any of the acts within the scope of this chapter has been guilty of any of the following: (a) Making any substantial misrepresentation. (b) Making any false promises of a character likely to influence, persuade, or induce. (c) A continued and flagrant course of misrepresentation or making of false promises through licensees. (d) Acting for more than one party in a transaction without the knowledge or consent of all parties thereto. (e) Commingling with h
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original strict prohibition against circumvention by introducing a subjective standard, "will try to avoid circumventing… to the best of their ability." This contradicts the intent of BPC 10176, which aims to prevent dishonest dealing by real estate licensees. While Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly mention circumvention, it broadly allows the commissioner to investigate actions of real estate licensees and take disciplinary action if they are guilty of making misrepresentations or false promises. The altered text makes it more difficult to prove dishonest dealing, as the buyer could argue they tried their best to avoid circumvention, even if their actions negatively impacted the seller or broker.

perturbation.original_text: In any action or proceeding, whether or not re sulting in litigation, between Buyer (or either of them) and Seller, or between Buyer (or either of them) and Broker, including any litigation to enforce any of the terms of this agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover, in addition to any damages or compensation received, its costs and expenses incurred in connection with such action or proceeding, including any reasonable attorneys ’ fees, expenses and court costs.
perturbation.changed_text: In any action or proceeding between Buyer and Seller, or between Buyer and Broker, the prevailing party may be entitled to some recovery of costs and expenses, including attorneys’ fees, depending on the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that the prevailing party *shall* be entitled to recover all reasonable costs and attorney fees. The modified text uses "may be entitled to some recovery," making the recovery of costs and fees discretionary and uncertain. The phrase "depending on the circumstances" further introduces ambiguity, leaving the scope of recovery undefined.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1033.5 details the items allowable as costs to a prevailing party in litigation. While it does allow for judicial discretion in certain situations, it also establishes a framework for recovering reasonable costs. The modified text contradicts this by making cost recovery highly discretionary ('may be entitled to some recovery') and vague ('depending on the circumstances'), potentially undermining the prevailing party's ability to recover costs as intended by the statute. The original clause more closely aligns with the intent of the statute by mandating recovery of reasonable costs and attorney fees as determined by the court.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1033.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1033.5. (a) The following items are allowable as costs under Section 1032: (1) Filing, motion, and jury fees. (2) Juror food and lodging while they are kept together during trial and after the jury retires for deliberation. (3) (A) Taking, video recording, and transcribing necessary depositions, including an original and one copy of those taken by the claimant and one copy of depositions taken by the party against whom costs are allowed. (B) Fees of a certified or registered interpreter for the deposition of a party or witness who does not proficiently speak or understand the English language. (C) Travel expenses to attend depositions. (4) Service of process by a public officer, registered process server, or other means, as follows: (A) When service is by a public officer, the recoverable cost is the fee authorized by law at the time of service. (B) If service is by a process server registered pursuant to Chapter 16 (commencing with Section 22350) of Division 8 of the Business and Professions Code, the recoverable cost is the amount actually i
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates the prevailing party *shall* recover costs and attorney fees. The modified text changes this to *may* recover *some* costs, depending on circumstances. This introduces ambiguity and discretion where the original text was definitive. CCP 1033.5(a) outlines allowable costs under Section 1032. While discretion exists in cost allowances, the original contract aligns more closely with the intent of cost recovery for the prevailing party. The modified text undermines the prevailing party's ability to recover costs, making it highly discretionary, creating a legal ambiguity that could lead to unenforceability.
